GB1-W01-A1 - Escape from Side Mount with.body:
## Concept

# Escape from Side Mount with Punches + Recovering Guard + Technical Lift

**Position:** Side Control  
**Category:** Defense  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 1 A1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

When pinned in side control and the opponent is throwing punches (self-defense or sport context), use frames to manage the strikes, create space with hip escape, recover guard, and finish with a technical lift if the opportunity arises or to improve position.

GB1 emphasizes active framing, hip movement to create angle, and the technical lift as a high-percentage way to come to top or at least recover a dominant guard.

## Setup

You are in bottom side control. Opponent has cross-face or underhook, chest heavy, and begins throwing punches.

- Create frames immediately (forearm on neck/hip, shin or knee in the way).
- Hips active — do not stay flat.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly connects to [[GB1-W01-B1 - Escape Mount with Punches + Bridge + Posture from Closed Guard]] (similar punch defense + bridge/hip escape principles).
- Guard recovery leads into [[GB1-W01-A2 - Pulling to Closed Guard Using Foot on Hip]] and the closed guard attack system we have enriched in later weeks.
- Technical lift mechanics overlap with the Week 5 block punches technical lift cards.

## Execution

### Frame and Manage Punches
1. Immediately post your near-side forearm across their neck or hip to create a barrier. Use your far hand to control their posting arm or head if possible.
2. Bring your near knee up into their hip or belly (knee shield / frame) to prevent them from settling heavy chest pressure.

### Hip Escape + Create Space
1. Bridge and shrimp (hip escape) away from their hips, creating angle and space under their chest.
2. Walk your shoulders/hips to create the angle while keeping frames tight against punches.
3. As space opens, bring your far leg in to hook or block their leg/hip.

### Recover Guard or Technical Lift
1. Once you have space, either pull them into your closed guard using the foot-on-hip or underhook pull (see A2 card), or use the technical lift: bridge high, post on your hand or elbow, and lift/sweep them over using your legs and hips.
2. Drive through the lift while maintaining control of their arm or posture.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Framing hand/forearm**: Near forearm across throat/shoulder or hip to jam punches and create the initial frame. Far hand controls their base arm or head.
- **Knee shield**: Bring the knee in to replace the frame as you shrimp — this is the transition from side control defense to guard recovery.
- **Technical lift base**: Post on the hand/elbow on the side you are escaping toward; use the far leg to hook their leg while the near leg and hips drive the lift. Weight shifts to the posting side during the bridge/lift (consistent with GB-reviewed hip mechanics in closed guard sweeps).

## Common Mistakes
- Staying flat and only pushing with arms (gets you hit and pinned heavier).
- Poor framing — reaching instead of posting the forearm across their structure.
- Not creating angle with the hip escape before attempting the lift (they base out easily).
- Losing posture control during the lift.

## Key Principles
- **Frame first against punches**: Never try to move until you have a barrier against the strikes.
- **Hip escape creates the angle**: The shrimp + shoulder walk is what makes the technical lift or guard pull possible — same hip movement principle as the GB Scottsdale triangle retention and sweep work (hip escape to re-create angle under pressure).
- **Technical lift as high-percentage recovery**: When the opponent is postured or overcommitted on punches, the bridge + lift often works better than pure guard pull because it uses their forward momentum.
- **Posting hip/base during the lift**: Maintain weight on the posting side while the active hip and legs drive — consistent GB affiliate hip loading across escapes and sweeps.
- **Chain to closed guard attacks**: Successfully recovering guard puts you immediately into the enriched closed guard system (X-Collar, triangle retention, sweeps) we have built in this campaign.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**

See the embedded video in the Media gallery above (Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist). This GB affiliate series covers Week 1 curriculum concepts including posture control with punches and guard recovery mechanics that align with the technical lift and framing emphasis in GB1.

Additional GB context from searches includes various GB school demonstrations of side control punch defense and guard recovery (Instagram reels from GB Denver, etc.). Specific long-form GB1 Week 1 side mount escape with punches from Scottsdale/Greensboro was limited; we used the affiliate playlist for the shared posture breaking, hip escape, and lift principles, cross-checked against standard GB1 mechanics.

**Why valuable:** The framing + hip escape + technical lift sequence is the direct application of the active hip and base principles we have reviewed in the GB Scottsdale closed guard and sweep content.

Cross-references: [[GB1-W01-B1 - Escape Mount with Punches + Bridge + Posture from Closed Guard]] and the guard pull A2 card for the connected Week 1 defense-to-guard system.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Strong forearm frame across neck/hip while opponent punches in side control.
- Knee shield replacement as you shrimp away.
- Hip escape creating angle under the opponent.
- Bridge + technical lift with posting hand and far leg hook.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The forearm frame + knee shield transition + posting hand during the lift are the critical details. These framing and hip escape mechanics are the foundation for all the side control and mount punch defense cards in Week 1 and later weeks. Drill together with the X-Collar posture breaking (B2/B3) and the closed guard recovery in A2.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W01-A2 - Pulling to Closed Guard Usi.body:
## Concept

# Pulling to Closed Guard Using Foot on Hip

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Guard Entry  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 1 A2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

A fundamental guard entry: when the opponent is in your side control or mount and you have created space (via hip escape or technical lift), use your foot on their hip to pull them into your closed guard while controlling their posture with the hands.

This is the direct bridge from side/mount defense into the closed guard attack system.

## Setup

You have created some space from side control or mount (via frames, hip escape, or technical lift). Opponent is postured or reaching.

- One foot is free to place on their hip or belt.
- Hands ready for collar or sleeve control.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct follow-up from [[GB1-W01-A1 - Escape from Side Mount with Punches + Recovering Guard + Technical Lift]] and [[GB1-W01-B1 - Escape Mount with Punches + Bridge + Posture from Closed Guard]].
- Leads immediately into the X-Collar (B2/B3) and the full closed guard sweep/attack family (Scissor A3, later weeks' triangle retention, etc.).
- Foot-on-hip mechanics appear in many guard pulls and sweeps throughout the curriculum.

## Execution

### Place the Foot on the Hip
1. As space opens (from your hip escape or their posture), bring your near foot up and place it firmly on their near hip or belt line (the "foot on hip" hook).
2. Use the foot to create a lever — push their hip away while pulling with your hands on collar or sleeve.

### Control Posture and Pull
1. With the foot creating distance on the hip, use your hands (collar tie, underhook, or sleeve) to break their posture forward or to the side.
2. Simultaneously pull with the foot on the hip while driving your other knee across or up to close the guard.
3. Once both legs are in, lock the closed guard and immediately work posture breaking (into X-Collar or other attacks).

### Hand Placement & References
- **Foot on hip**: The foot is placed flat on the hip/belt, toes pointed slightly up or across, creating a strong pushing surface. This is the primary lever for pulling them into guard.
- **Collar/sleeve control**: The opposite hand drives the collar or sleeve to break posture in the direction that helps the pull. Wrist position should be strong (flat or slightly supinated) — consistent with the X-Collar four-finger grip mechanics we have in B2.
- **Knee drive**: The free knee drives across or up to help close the guard as the foot pulls.

## Common Mistakes
- Weak foot placement (slips off the hip; no leverage).
- Only pulling with the arms without using the foot as the lever (they posture up and pass).
- Closing the guard without breaking their posture first (they settle heavy and pass easily).
- Not immediately transitioning to an attack (X-Collar or triangle threat) once in guard.

## Key Principles
- **Foot on hip is the lever**: The foot does the heavy pulling work; the hands control posture and direction. This is the fundamental guard pull mechanic in GB1 Week 1.
- **Posture break before closing**: Never lock the guard until you have broken their base — otherwise they pass or smash.
- **Hip escape creates the window**: The space from your earlier hip escape (A1 card) is what allows the foot to get to the hip cleanly.
- **Immediate attack transition**: Once in closed guard, you are in the enriched attack system (X-Collar B2/B3, triangle retention, sweeps) — do not stall in neutral guard.
- **Consistent lever mechanics**: The foot-on-hip + hand control is the same principle used in many later sweeps (pull feet, waiter, etc.).

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**

**Primary (exact GB1 curriculum match):** Gracie Barra Harrogate — "GB1 Wk1 Guard Pull" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfYa3BlgUIg). This is the precise Week 1 A2 technique from a GB affiliate school, matching the curriculum the user trains at Gracie Barra Greensboro.

**Additional high-value GB affiliate:** Professor Philipe Della Monica (GB HQ Irvine) — "Guard Pull Counter" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gstQuj57i_Y). Excellent instruction on the mechanics and reactions when pulling from side control or mount positions.

The GB Scottsdale playlist provides supporting context for early closed guard posture breaking that pairs with the pull.

**Why these are valuable:** The Harrogate video shows the exact foot placement, timing of the pull with posture break, and transition into closed guard attacks as taught in GB1. The HQ video adds depth on counters and setups from the defensive positions in A1 and B1.

Cross-references: The full Week 1 defense-to-guard system (A1 side mount escape, B1 mount escape) and the X-Collar cards (B2/B3) that you enter immediately after the pull.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Foot flat on the opponent's hip/belt with toes engaged.
- Hand driving collar or sleeve to break posture as the foot pulls.
- Knee driving across to help close the guard.
- Immediate transition into X-Collar or other attack once legs are locked.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The foot-on-hip lever + strong collar/sleeve drive is the defining placement for this entry. The wrist and grip mechanics here are the direct foundation for the X-Collar four-finger and thumb-in variations in B2/B3. Drill this pull together with the side/mount punch defense cards (A1, B1) and the scissor sweep (A3).

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W01-B1 - Escape Mount with Punches +.body:
## Concept

# Escape Mount with Punches + Bridge + Posture from Closed Guard

**Position:** Mount  
**Category:** Defense  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 1 B1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

When the opponent has mounted and is throwing punches, use the bridge and hip escape to create space, recover guard (often via the foot-on-hip pull), while managing the strikes with frames and posture control.

This is the mount version of the side control punch defense in A1, using the same active hip and framing principles.

## Setup

You are in bottom mount. Opponent has base, hands on your chest or throat, and begins punching.

- Create frames with forearms and hands.
- Hips must be active — do not stay flat on the mat.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct partner to [[GB1-W01-A1 - Escape from Side Mount with Punches + Recovering Guard + Technical Lift]] (same punch defense + hip escape + guard recovery theme).
- Guard recovery leads into [[GB1-W01-A2 - Pulling to Closed Guard Using Foot on Hip]] and the X-Collar attacks (B2/B3).
- Bridge and posture concepts overlap with later mount escapes and the Roy Dean stack counter work.

## Execution

### Frame and Protect Against Punches
1. Post both forearms across their hips, chest, or neck to create a barrier and jam the punches at the source.
2. Keep your elbows tight to your body; do not reach up into the strikes.

### Bridge + Hip Escape
1. Bridge explosively upward while shrimping one hip out to the side (create angle).
2. As you bridge, push with the frames to off-balance them forward or to the side.
3. Once you have space on one side, bring your knee in or foot up toward their hip.

### Recover Guard (Foot on Hip Pull)
1. With space created, place your foot on their hip (or use the technical lift variation) and pull them into your closed guard while driving your other knee across.
2. Immediately work posture breaking once the guard is locked (transition to X-Collar or other attacks).

### Hand Placement & References
- **Forearm frames**: Both forearms posted across their structure (hips, chest, or neck) to jam punches and create the initial lift for the bridge.
- **Foot on hip during recovery**: Same lever as the A2 card — once space is made, the foot becomes the primary tool to pull them into guard.
- **Posting during bridge**: One hand or elbow posts on the mat on the side you are shrimping toward, providing the base for the explosive bridge (consistent with technical lift posting in A1 and GB-reviewed hip mechanics).

## Common Mistakes
- Bridging straight up without shrimping to the side (they base and stay heavy).
- Weak frames — getting hit while trying to move.
- Not using the foot-on-hip pull once space is created (miss the guard recovery window).
- Losing the angle and letting them settle back into mount.

## Key Principles
- **Frame the punches, then move**: Never attempt the bridge/escape until you have a barrier against the strikes.
- **Bridge + shrimp creates the angle**: The explosive bridge combined with the hip escape to the side is what makes the guard pull or lift possible — same hip movement as the GB Scottsdale triangle retention and sweep work.
- **Foot on hip as the finisher**: Once space exists, the foot lever pulls them into the closed guard system we have enriched.
- **Posting base for power**: The posting hand/elbow during the bridge provides the stability to generate the lift against a larger opponent.
- **Immediate transition to attack**: Successfully recovering guard puts you into the X-Collar and triangle retention material — do not stall.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**

See the embedded video in the Media gallery above (Gracie Barra Scottsdale playlist). The series includes mount escape bridge instruction (Week 4 headlock escape, Week 16 front choke escape) that shares the bridge, hip escape, framing, and posture breaking principles with this Week 1 punch defense mount escape.

Additional GB context from searches includes various GB school demonstrations of mount punch defense and guard recovery. Specific long-form GB1 Week 1 mount escape with punches from the main affiliates was limited; we used the affiliate series for the shared hip and framing mechanics, cross-checked against standard GB1 bridge escape details.

**Why valuable:** The bridge + hip escape + guard pull sequence is the direct application of the active hip principles reviewed across the GB Scottsdale closed guard and sweep content.

Cross-references: [[GB1-W01-A1 - Escape from Side Mount with Punches + Recovering Guard + Technical Lift]] and [[GB1-W01-A2 - Pulling to Closed Guard Using Foot on Hip]] for the complete Week 1 defense-to-guard system.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Double forearm frame across hips/chest while mounted and being punched.
- Explosive bridge with hip shrimp to one side.
- Foot on hip pull as space opens.
- Recovery into closed guard with immediate posture break (X-Collar entry).

**Hand Placement & References**  
The forearm frames + posting hand during bridge + foot-on-hip pull are the critical details. These are the same framing and hip escape mechanics as the side control version (A1) and the guard pull (A2). Drill together with the X-Collar cards (B2/B3) that you enter immediately after recovery.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W01-B2 - X-Collar Choke (Four Finger.body:
## Concept

# X-Collar Choke (Four Fingers)

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Submission  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 1 B2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The four-finger X-Collar (cross-collar) choke is one of the most reliable and accessible submissions from closed guard. It teaches critical collar feeding, posture breaking, and attacking the neck from the inside. The four-finger grip provides strong control, especially against larger or stronger opponents.

This is the direct follow-up attack once you have pulled the opponent into closed guard (A2 card).

## Setup

You are in closed guard (recently recovered via A2 or from other entries). Opponent has some posture (hands on your hips or chest, head up).

- Secure a strong collar grip on one side (the far collar for the choke).
- Use your other hand to control posture (sleeve, tricep, or head).
- Hips active and ready to elevate.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct follow-up from [[GB1-W01-A2 - Pulling to Closed Guard Using Foot on Hip]] (the guard entry that sets up this attack).
- Paired with [[GB1-W01-B3 - X-Collar Choke (Thumb-In)]] — learn both variations.
- Posture breaking and collar feed overlap with [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] and the Roy Dean stack counter.
- Grip concepts transfer to many closed guard attacks (Kimura, armbar transitions).

## Execution

### Grip & Feed (Four Fingers)
1. Reach deep into the far collar with your choking hand (four fingers in, thumb out for maximum purchase).
2. Your choking hand's wrist should be straight or slightly flexed.
3. Use your other hand to pull the opponent forward (collar tie or underhook) while driving your knees toward your chest to lift their hips and break posture.

### Finish
1. Once posture is broken, bring your second hand to the opposite collar (creating the "X").
2. Pull both elbows to your hips/sides while walking your shoulders toward the opponent's far hip.
3. Squeeze with the legs and keep your own hips off the mat.
4. The choke tightens as you pull the collars across the neck and elevate slightly with the hips.

### Hand Placement & References
- Choking hand: Four fingers deep in the collar, thumb outside, wrist flat. This is the strongest grip for larger opponents.
- Supporting hand: Reaches across to the far collar. The thumb of the supporting hand often ends up pointing toward the opponent's ear or shoulder.
- Compare to the Thumb-In variation (B3) — the wrist angle and pressure points differ slightly.
- This grip mechanic appears in the Kimura from closed guard and certain back control chokes.

## Common Mistakes
- Shallow collar grip (only two fingers) — slips under pressure.
- Collapsing the wrist on the choking hand (loses leverage).
- Only pulling the head instead of elevating the opponent's hips with your knees/legs first.
- Letting your own hips stay flat on the mat during the finish.
- Crossing the arms too early before posture is sufficiently broken.

## Key Principles
- **Posture before submission**: Break the opponent's base and flatten their posture before committing to the choke finish (core GB1 teaching).
- **Wrist straight = power**: A flat wrist on the four-finger grip transmits force directly into the neck.
- **Hips elevate the opponent**: Use your closed guard to lift their hips toward you — this makes their head drop and the neck more vulnerable. Same hip elevation principle reviewed in GB Scottsdale closed guard and sweep content.
- **X-grip creates the cross**: The two collars must cross the neck centerline for the choke to be effective.
- **Legs active**: Squeeze and elevate with the legs throughout the finish — do not rely on arm strength alone.
- **Foundational for the closed guard attack system**: This is the first major submission taught in GB1 closed guard; the collar feed, posture break, and hip elevation carry forward to triangle, armbar, and kimura.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**

See the embedded video in the Media gallery above (Gracie Barra Scottsdale playlist). The affiliate series provides supporting instruction on early closed guard collar attacks and the posture breaking that sets up the X-Collar.

The core mechanics (deep collar feed, wrist position, hip elevation for posture break) are consistent with the GB1 curriculum and the high-fidelity GB-style sources we have used throughout this campaign (e.g., prior detailed collar and posture work).

**Why valuable:** Demonstrates the four-finger feed and elbow drive to the hips that make the choke reliable against resistance — directly supports the "Posture before submission" and "Hips elevate the opponent" principles.

Cross-references: [[GB1-W01-B3 - X-Collar Choke (Thumb-In)]] for the variation, [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] for the shared posture breaking and hip elevation, and the guard pull A2 card for the entry that sets this attack up.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Close-up of four fingers fully inserted in the collar with thumb outside and flat wrist.
- Side angle showing the X formed by the two arms across the neck during the finish.
- Hip elevation moment (knees driving toward chest while pulling collars).

**Hand Placement & References**  
The four-finger collar grip is the foundation for many closed guard submissions. See [[GB1-W01-B3 - X-Collar Choke (Thumb-In)]] for the alternative grip and when to choose each. The wrist control and elbow drive concepts transfer directly to the Triangle from Closed Guard and Armbar from Guard finishes.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W01-B3 - X-Collar Choke (Thumb-In).m.body:
## Concept

# X-Collar Choke (Thumb-In)

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Submission  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 1 B3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The thumb-in X-Collar variation offers a different grip feel and can be tighter against opponents who posture aggressively or have thick necks. The thumb inside allows for a slightly different wrist angle and can create more direct pressure on the carotid.

Understanding both variations (with B2) gives you options based on grip strength, opponent size, and gi thickness.

This is the direct follow-up attack once you have pulled the opponent into closed guard (A2 card).

## Setup

Same as the Four Fingers version: closed guard, opponent with base and posture.

- Often entered when the opponent is already somewhat broken down or when you want a tighter, more direct neck attack.
- The thumb-in grip is frequently used when transitioning from other collar attacks or when the four-finger grip is slipping.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly paired with [[GB1-W01-B2 - X-Collar Choke (Four Fingers)]] — learn both to choose the right tool.
- Grip concepts overlap with [[GB1-W13-A3 - Kimura from Closed Guard]] and many back control chokes.
- Posture breaking overlaps with the triangle retention cluster.

## Execution

### Grip & Feed (Thumb-In)
1. Feed the choking hand deep into the collar with the thumb inside (thumb points toward the opponent's chest/neck).
2. The thumb provides extra leverage and can help "wedge" the collar deeper.
3. Use the opposite hand for posture control (collar tie, sleeve, or head) while driving your knees to your chest to break posture.

### Finish
1. Once posture is broken, bring the second hand to the opposite collar (creating the "X").
2. Pull both elbows to your hips/sides while walking your shoulders toward the opponent's far hip.
3. Squeeze with the legs and keep your own hips off the mat. The choke tightens as you pull the collars across the neck and elevate slightly with the hips.

### Hand Placement & References
- Choking hand: Thumb inside the collar, fingers outside, wrist position that allows the thumb to drive pressure. This can create a more direct carotid compression on certain body types.
- Supporting hand: Reaches across to the far collar. The supporting thumb often ends up pointing toward the opponent's ear or shoulder.
- Compare to the Four Fingers variation (B2) — the wrist angle and pressure points differ slightly; choose based on opponent and gi.
- This grip mechanic appears in certain back control chokes and can transition into kimura threats.

## Common Mistakes
- Shallow feed (thumb not deep enough) — loses the wedging advantage.
- Wrong wrist angle (collapsing or over-extending the thumb).
- Only pulling the head instead of elevating the hips first.
- Crossing the arms too early.
- Letting your own hips stay flat.

## Key Principles
- **Posture before submission**: Same as B2 — break base and flatten posture before finishing.
- **Thumb as wedge**: The inside thumb helps drive the collar deeper and can increase direct pressure on the neck.
- **Hips elevate the opponent**: The closed guard does the heavy lifting; the hands apply the finishing pressure. Same hip elevation principle reviewed in GB Scottsdale closed guard content.
- **Choose the right variation**: Four-finger for power against larger opponents; thumb-in for tighter feel or when the four-finger is slipping. Both are essential tools in the GB1 closed guard system.
- **Foundational grip for the curriculum**: The collar feed and posture break here carry forward to triangle entries, kimura from guard, and many back attacks.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**

See the embedded video in the Media gallery above (Gracie Barra Scottsdale playlist). The affiliate series provides supporting instruction on early closed guard collar attacks and the posture breaking that sets up both X-Collar variations.

The core mechanics (deep feed, posture break with hip elevation, X-grip finish) are consistent with the GB1 curriculum and the high-fidelity GB-style sources used throughout this campaign.

**Why valuable:** Demonstrates the thumb-in feed and the slight difference in wrist angle/pressure compared to the four-finger version, helping you choose the right tool in live rolling.

Cross-references: [[GB1-W01-B2 - X-Collar Choke (Four Fingers)]] for the primary variation, [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] for the shared posture breaking, and the guard pull A2 card for the entry.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Close-up of thumb inside the collar with fingers outside and the wedging action.
- Comparison side-by-side of four-finger vs thumb-in wrist positions.
- Hip elevation moment with the X-grip finish.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The thumb-in collar grip is the alternative tool to the four-finger version. See [[GB1-W01-B2 - X-Collar Choke (Four Fingers)]] for the primary grip. The wrist control and elbow drive concepts transfer directly to the Triangle from Closed Guard and Kimura from Closed Guard finishes.
